title: Synchronized molecular dynamics method for thin-layer flows of complex fluids
authors: Shugo Yasuda, Kotaro Oda, Fumito Muragaki, Yuta Taketa, Masashi Iwayama, Tomohide Ina
abstract: We propose a multiscale computational method for thin-layer flows of complex fluids, termed the synchronized molecular dynamics (SMD) method, which directly couples local molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with a macroscopic lubrication description. In thin layers, the flow can be decomposed into cross-sectional dynamics that are strongly influenced by interfacial effects, and streamwise transport along the channel. The SMD method exploits this separation of scales by sparsely distributing local MD cells along the channel and synchronizing them through macroscopic conservation laws.   In this framework, the macroscopic continuity equation is enforced by iteratively updating the external forces applied to each MD cell, thereby allowing the cross-sectional velocity profiles and the streamwise pressure distribution to be obtained without prescribing constitutive relations or boundary conditions. The method is validated for pressure-driven and wall-driven flows of Lennard--Jones fluids in a wedge-shaped channel, demonstrating excellent agreement with a modified Reynolds equation that accounts for boundary slip.   The SMD method is further applied to polymeric lubrication flows modeled by the Kremer--Grest chain model. At large pressure differences, the present approach naturally captures pronounced shear-thinning behavior coupled with microscopic polymer conformation dynamics. The results demonstrate that the SMD method provides an efficient and physically consistent framework for the multiscale simulation of complex fluid thin-layer flows.
summary: This paper introduces the Synchronized Molecular Dynamics (SMD) method, a multiscale computational technique for simulating complex fluid flows in thin channels. By coupling local molecular dynamics simulations with a macroscopic lubrication model, the method bypasses the need for predefined constitutive equations. It is validated against known Newtonian models and successfully demonstrates the ability to capture complex phenomena like shear-thinning and polymer stretching. This provides an efficient way to study micro-to-macro transport properties in complex fluids.
main_problem: Predicting the flow of complex fluids (polymers, colloids) in thin-layer geometries where constitutive relations and boundary conditions, such as slip, are unknown and difficult to establish.
main_result: The proposed SMD method successfully captures shear-thinning and polymer conformation dynamics in polymeric flows and validates against modified Reynolds equations for Newtonian fluids.
method: A multiscale Synchronized Molecular Dynamics (SMD) method that couples local MD cells with a macroscopic lubrication description by iteratively updating external forces to satisfy continuity.
model_or_system: Thin-layer/film flows in high-aspect-ratio channels (e.g., wedge-shaped) using Lennard-Jones fluids for validation and the Kremer-Grest chain model for polymeric liquids.
key_observables: Cross-sectional velocity profiles, streamwise pressure distribution, polymer conformation dynamics (bond orientation tensor), and slip velocity/length.
important_parameters: Reynolds number (Re << 1), aspect ratio (epsilon << 1), Weissenberg number, fluid density, and fluid-wall interaction strength.
main_assumptions: Laminar, incompressible flow in the lubrication limit (low Reynolds number and thin-layer geometry) where scales can be separated.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the MD cell geometry; Figure 2 illustrates the SMD iteration scheme; Figure 3 shows the MPI parallelization hierarchy; Figures 9-13 present error analysis, force distributions, and velocity profiles for polymeric vs. Newtonian fluids.
paper_structure: Introduction of the problem -> Description of the SMD multiscale framework and implementation -> Validation using LJ fluids and slip analysis -> Application to polymeric fluids and non-Newtonian effects -> Appendix with lubrication theory derivation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
